role_summary: Second executive director of the Northern Student Movement (1963-1965); northern coordinator of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's 1965 congressional challenge; co-founder of the Institute of the Black World (1969); professor of political science in the W.E.B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies at the University of Massachusetts Amherst (1973-2013).
ai_reading: By the precomputed neighbor counts the page renders from /rag/related/entry-127.json, Strickland's closest passages in this corpus belong to Phil Hutchings (entry 98, count 134), Junius Williams (entry 72, count 128), John Churchville (entry 63, count 127), Evans Derrell Hopkins (entry 42, count 117), and Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53, count 114). The cluster is the post-1965 Black Power organizing cohort, and on the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis his normalized position is -0.76 toward Strategic Vision while his Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle position is -0.58 toward Northern. The Strategic Vision pole tracks the long-horizon, structural-analysis register of his record, the Institute of the Black World research program and the decades of political-science teaching, and the Northern pole tracks the cultural placement of him as a northern organizer, so the page carries no AI-vs-culture inversion on its axes. The one neighbor the historiography does not foreground is Evans Derrell Hopkins (entry 42), a Black Panther Party associate who wrote from prison: the embedding ranks Hopkins's testimony fourth among Strickland's neighbors, ahead of several SNCC contemporaries, even though Strickland on record says he had a problem with the Panthers. The cosine space appears to be reading the shared post-1965 vocabulary of state violence, policing, and political imprisonment rather than the organizational line that separated the two men.
biographical_paragraph: William Lamar Strickland was born in Roxbury, Massachusetts, on January 4, 1937, and raised by his mother, Mittie Louise Strickland, a union worker whose family had moved north from Georgia [src: 5]. He graduated from Boston Latin School and Harvard, served in the United States Marine Corps from 1956 to 1959, and in September 1963 succeeded Peter Countryman as the second executive director of the Northern Student Movement, the northern campus organization that ran tutoring and community projects in Boston, New York, Hartford, Chicago, and Detroit [src: 3]. In 1964 and 1965 he coordinated the northern congressional support for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge that sought to unseat the state's white representatives [src: 1]. After the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr., Strickland co-founded the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta with Vincent Harding in 1969, the independent Black research center he describes operating until 1983 [src: 2]. He joined the W.E.B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies at the University of Massachusetts Amherst in 1973, taught political science there until his retirement in 2013, and directed the W.E.B. Du Bois Papers [src: 2]. He served as a consultant on the documentary series Eyes on the Prize and on the 1994 PBS film Malcolm X: Make It Plain, and wrote the companion volume to that film [src: 2]. In his oral history, held by the Library of Congress as entry 127 of the Civil Rights History Project, Strickland names other figures in this corpus, including Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34), whom he places as the Washington organizer for the MFDP challenge, and Junius Williams (entry 72), whose Newark organizing he discusses alongside his own [src: 1]. He died at his home in Amherst on April 10, 2024 [src: 4].

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Tracing how he entered organized movement work while a student in Cambridge:
interview_snippets.quote: I was at Harvard, and that's when I, um, joined Northern Student Movement

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the lesson he took from Harlem rent-strike organizer Jesse Gray about how organizing actually works:
interview_snippets.quote: the secret of organizing is, is, is convincing people to organize themselves, although they may not realize that it's what's happening at the time.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Placing Malcolm X, whom he knew personally, in a line of fundamental critics:
interview_snippets.quote: To my mind, Malcolm X is the fundamental critic of American racism.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Arguing that the turn to Black Power followed from repeated federal failure to protect movement workers, not from a change of mood:
interview_snippets.quote: I mean black power doesn't drop from the sky.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling what the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party work left him with, sitting with Fannie Lou Hamer:
interview_snippets.quote: there were people like Mrs. [Fannie Lou] Hamer's who could see the world with a clarity that was infinitely superior to mine.

sources.title: William Lamar Strickland oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Amherst, Massachusetts, 2011 September 23
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (American Folklife Center, AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Memorial Tribute in Honor of Prof. Bill Strickland

Du Bois Papers directorship, the 1969 Institute of the Black World co-founding with Vincent Harding, and his Eyes on the Prize and Malcolm X: Make It Plain consultancies)

gallery.alt: Black-and-white portrait of Malcolm X wearing glasses, photographed in March 1964.
gallery.caption: Malcolm X in March 1964. Strickland knew Malcolm X personally, calls him the fundamental critic of American racism in his oral history, and later wrote the companion volume to the PBS film Malcolm X: Make It Plain.
gallery.photographer: Ed Ford, New York World-Telegram and Sun staff photographer
